videos.title: Perplexity Just Dropped Their Own OpenClaw And It Hits Hard
videos.description: Perplexity just released Perplexity Computer, a system built to push AI beyond search and into long-running autonomous work. Instead of answering prompts step by step, it executes full workflows end to end, handling research, planning, writing, coding, deployment, revisions, and ongoing management inside one coordinated environment. Models are treated as interchangeable tools, dynamically routed across reasoning, research, image, video, and fast-response tasks to keep work moving without constan
videos.transcript: Perplexity went quiet for months, then suddenly showed up with Perplexity Computer. The move that pushes them far beyond search and straight into the AI worker race. The same space tools like OpenClaw have been heating up. The idea is an AI that runs full projects on its own, keeps working for hours or days, and handles real tasks end to end instead of waiting around for the next prompt. The company is very clear about the shift they're making here. Chat interfaces answer questions. agents do tasks. Perplexity computer is meant to execute entire workflows from start to finish. Not just one step, not just one tool, but the full chain. Research, planning, writing, coding, deployment, revisions, and ongoing management. You describe an outcome you want and the system figures out how to get there. Aravan Shrinavas, Perplexity's CEO, said they've been building this quietly for months. His framing is interesting because he doesn't talk about model power as the main limitation anymore. His argument is that models are already strong, but using them effectively in isolation has become the real bottleneck. Perplexity Computer is their attempt to fix that by turning multiple models into something that behaves like a coordinated system instead of a single brain answering prompts. The core idea is that Perplexity Computer operates the same interfaces humans do. It uses a real browser, a real file system, real APIs, real tools. Every task runs inside its own isolated compute environment. So when it researches something, writes code, generates visuals, or processes data, it's actually doing that work in a sandboxed environment with proper access and controls. The workflow starts very simply. You tell it what outcome you want, not how to do it, just what the end result should be. The system maps out the work on its own, splits it into smaller steps, and assigns those steps across specialized AI agents. Research, writing, coding, visuals, data handling, and tool integrations all run in parallel inside the same workflow. The coordination happens automatically in the background, so the work keeps moving forward while you step away instead of sitting there watching progress bars. One of the more important technical details is that Perplexity Computer is multimodel by design. As of launch, it can orchestrate work across 19 different AI models. There isn't one single model doing everything. Instead, different models are selected dynamically depending on the task. Reasoning tasks go to a reasoning focused model. Research tasks go to a research optimized model. Image generation goes to an image model. Video tasks go to a video model. Lightweight or fast operations can be routed to models optimized for speed. At launch, the core reasoning engine is Opus 4.6. Research tasks can be handled by Gemini. Images are handled by Nano Banana. Video generation can use VO 3.1. Grock is used for lighter fast tasks. Chat GPT 5.2 is used for long context recall and search. The key point here is not which models are used, but the fact that perplexity treats models as interchangeable components. Sinovas explicitly said that when models specialize, they become tools similar to a file system, command line tools, connectors, browsers, or search. That philosophy is a big shift. Quick pause here because this shift to AI run work is already playing out in the real world. Someone just won $1 million with an AI film made using V3 on stage at the 1 billion followers summit hosted by the UAE government. A 19-year-old runs an AI automated agency making $100,000 a month. Forb says employees using AI earn 40% more. The AI job shift is here and the early adopter phase is over. That's why outskill is getting attention. They're hosting a live 2-day AI mastermind training this weekend, 10:00 a.m. to 700 p.m. EST both days, and you can join free for the next 48 hours. This 16-hour training has helped build over a million AI first professionals worldwide and holds a 4.9 out of five rating on Trustpilot. You'll learn to build AI agents, automate workflows, connect tools, save hours every week, and turn these skills into real income. Attend both days, and you also unlock premium bonuses, and the 2026 AI survival hackbook. Links in the description. All right, now back to the video. Instead of competing by saying our model is better than yours, perplexity is betting that the real moat is orchestration. Knowing which model to use, when to use it, how to chain them together, and how to manage the outputs into something coherent. Sinavas even pointed out that in 2025, a new frontier model launched roughly every 17 days. In that kind of environment, the advantage shifts away from owning a single model and towards scheduling heterogeneous models effectively. What this enables is longunning work. Perplexity computer can run workflows for hours, days, or even months. If it runs into a problem, it can spawn additional sub aents to troubleshoot and solve that problem. It keeps track of progress. It can pull in new information over time. It doesn't require you to babysit it with constant follow-up prompts. This is where the AI is the computer framing starts to make sense. Perplexity is positioning this as a reimagining of what a personal computer should be in 2026. A system that's personal to you, remembers your past work, has persistent memory, and is secure by default. Instead of clicking through a graphical interface and manually switching between tools, the AI itself becomes the thing orchestrating the tools. There's also a very clear professional and enterprise angle here. At launch, Perplexity Computer is only available to Mac subscribers. The pricing model is usagebased with monthly credits and optional spending caps. Max subscribers get 10,000 credits per month, plus a one-time bonus of 20,000 credits that expire after 30 days. You can choose which models to use for specific sub aents, set token limits, and manage budgets. Enterprise and prolevel access is planned to follow after testing. Because this all runs inside Perplexity's managed environment. The company controls the infrastructure, the integrations, and the safeguards. That's a big contrast with open-source agent systems, and it brings us directly to the OpenClaw comparison that's been popping up everywhere. Perplexity Computer is actually Perplexity's direct answer to OpenClaw, which has been growing rapidly in the open source world. OpenClaw, originally called Claudebot before a trademark dispute, runs locally on a user's machine. It can connect to email, messaging platforms, and local files. It can execute commands and automate workflows with deep system access. Users choose which models to connect and how much control to grant the agent. That flexibility is exactly why developers love OpenClaw. At the same time, it shifts responsibility for configuration and security onto the user. Security researchers have already warned that misconfigured agents with deep system access can introduce serious vulnerabilities, including unauthorized command execution. Now, Perplexity is taking the opposite approach. Computer runs in a centralized managed environment. Perplexity hosts everything. They manage updates. They impose safeguards. They monitor performance. For enterprises, that creates clearer lines of responsibility and accountability. You define the objective. Perplexity defines how the system interacts with websites, applications, and external services. This difference matters because both products represent the same underlying shift. Agents are no longer just answering questions on a screen. They're operating through application interfaces, performing work that used to require human oversight. Whether you prefer a fully managed system or a self-hosted open-source agent depends on your priorities, but the direction is the same. What really pushed this over the edge was how Perplexity talked about the launch itself. Some coverage went pretty wild, calling it 19 top AIs storming Silicon Valley and turning expensive tools into junk. That sounds dramatic. Yet, there's a real point behind it. With multiple models working together, Perplexity Computer can spin up a live financial analysis system that lands in Bloomberg territory. And that matters because Bloomberg terminals run around $30,000 a year for real-time data analytics and expert tooling. This system can analyze stocks, generate charts, summarize financials, and pull market insights in one continuous workflow. It doesn't stop there. The same setup can dig through podcasts, find the exact moment someone like Daario Amodi talks about model differentiation, clip it, edit it into a vertical video, add subtitles, and prep it for Tik Tok. It can also handle practical stuff like building a full return on investment model for turning a specific property into a short-term rental. A lot of this traces back to Alex Graveley, the former chief architect of GitHub Copilot, who's been at Perplexity for the past two years and also built their AI native browser, Comet. That's why this feels less like a chatbot and more like an operating layer. Sinovas went even further in a long post tied to the launch where he talked about an internal experiment called ASI. It started as a Slack plugin and evolved into a system that could break down work on its own, spin up sub agents, call hundreds of tools, and write code inside a cloud sandbox. His point was simple. AI has moved past the chat box and into the role of the computer itself, coordinating files, tools, and logic in the background. Now, while all of this was happening on the software side, Perplexity was also quietly expanding its footprint on hardware. Samsung announced that Perplexity is being integrated directly into upcoming Galaxy S26 phones. Users will be able to wake Perplexity using the phrase, "Hey Plex," putting it right alongside Google's Gemini Assistant on Android devices. Perplexity's sonar API is powering parts of Samsung's Galaxy AI ecosystem. Perplexity's engineers worked closely with Samsung to revamp Bixby at the framework level, giving Perplexity deep system access. According to Perplexity's chief business officer, Dimmitri Chevaleno, this is the first time a third-party AI company has achieved par with Google on a major mobile operating system. Perplexity's integration touches core Samsung apps like calendar, clock, gallery, notes, reminders, and even the Samsung internet browser, which now includes agentic browsing powered by Perplexity's Comet technology. The idea is that interaction becomes lighter. Instead of unlocking your phone, navigating screens, opening apps, and typing queries, you press a button or say a wake word and the agent handles the task across apps. This fits perfectly with their broader strategy because they don't want to build their own phone. They've been very clear about that. Instead, they want to be the AI layer embedded across the best devices and platforms. The same approach showed up earlier with Deutsche Telecom's AI phone in 2025, where Perplexity powered the core agent experience. And that's where I'll leave it. This is still early. It's still evolving, but the direction is clear. Thanks for watching, and I'll catch you in the next one.

videos.title: Everything You Need to Know About OpenClaw
videos.description:
OpenClaw just hit 150,000+ GitHub stars and became the most talked-about AI tool of 2026. But what exactly is it? In this video, I break down everything you need to know — what OpenClaw is, how to set it up on a cloud server, real use cases people are running right now (some are genuinely insane), and the serious security risks you NEED to understand before diving in.
OpenClaw is a free, open-source AI agent that runs locally on your machine and connects to WhatsApp, Telegram, or Discord — turni
videos.transcript: What if I told you you could text your AI assistant on WhatsApp or iMessage right now and say, "Hey, clear my inbox, check my calendar, and send me a summary." And it would actually do it. Not just give you instructions to do it, but actually do it on its own. That's not sci-fi, that's OpenClaw. And it just became the most talked about AI tool of 2026, hitting over 150,000 GitHub stars in just a few months. So today I'm breaking down everything that you need to know about open claw. What it is, how it works, what people are actually doing with it. Some of it are genuinely jaw-dropping and also the risks because there are some real ones that you really need to know about. So let's get started. Okay, so you have used chat GPT or claude, right? You ask it something, it gives you a great answer. Maybe it writes an email or explains a concept. That's awesome. But here's the thing, it stops at giving you the answer and you still have to do the actual work yourself. Open claw is different. It doesn't just give you suggestions. It actually executes things on your behalf. Think of it like this. Chipd is like having a really smart advisor sitting across from you. OpenClaw is like having that smart advisor but they also have the access to your computer, your email, your calendar, your files and they can actually do stuff for you. It runs locally meaning on your machine or a server that you control and then you connect it to your messaging app like WhatsApp, Telegram, Discord, whatever you prefer. And from that point on, it's basically your personal AI employee that you can text at any time. So, OpenClaw was built by an Austrian developer named Peter Stainberger, a well-known Mac developer, and it blew up almost overnight. The project went through a few name changes, Clawbot, then Moldbot, and after some trademark dispute with Anthropic. It finally landed on OpenClaw. It's completely free and open source. The only cost is whatever AI model that you connected to like paying for claude or Chad GPT API access. Claw co-work is something that comes close to doing your task on its behalf. But OpenClaw takes it to a step further and does a lot more things that co-work could not. I'll explain this in a bit. But first, let me show you directly how to set it up and how to let it do the work for you. First, you need a server or a computer to run it on. You can use your own laptop or PC, but the better move and what most people do is use a dedicated machine just for running OpenClaw. Many people have gone out and bought a Mac Mini just to run OpenC claw. What I'm going to do today is get a Linux VM on Azure. Basically, I'm renting a computer from Microsoft cloud to run Open Claw on it. It could be an Azure VM, a digital Ocean droplet, an AWS light sale instance, or even an old laptop of yours. It's just better for security, and that way it's always running 24x7 and is also secure. Now I'm on my Azure VM. It's running Ubuntu. Now for the OpenCloud setup, it's super simple. It's just this one line. It handles everything. NodeJS, all the dependencies, all of it automatically. After that, an interactive setup wizard walks you through everything. You pick your AI model. I use Claude because it's actually recommended by OpenClaw for being better at handling security. You connect your messaging app and you're all done. For WhatsApp specifically, it shows you a QR code. You can scan it from your phone exactly like you would with WhatsApp web and boom, you're linked. After that, you just text your own number on WhatsApp and your AI responds. I know that sounds wild, but it actually works exactly like that. The whole thing takes maybe 15 to 20 minutes. Honestly, less if you have done any kind of server setup before. Okay, this is the part of the video that made me want to make this video because when I started digging into what people are actually doing with Open Claw, I was genuinely blown away. I'll show you three use cases where you can use Open Claw to let it do the work for you. The simplest and the most popular one, a personalized morning briefing. Every morning before you even pick up your phone, OpenClaw texts you the weather, your calendar events for the day, and the top news headlines. How did I set it up? I just ask OpenClaw on my WhatsApp to report me these things every day at 8:00 a.m. OpenClaw will take care of pulling the data from the web, setting up a job that runs every morning and all of that all automatically. All I need to do is just give the respective permissions and enable the skills needed for doing that job. Next, you drop a meeting recording into a chat. It transcribes it, identifies who said what, pulls out all the action items, and sends you a clean summary. That alone would cost you a subscription to most other tools. One of the most popular things people do with OpenClaw is the overnight research agent. Here's how it actually works. You enable web search skill and the file manager skill from OpenClaw hub. That's literally just two terminal commands. Then you message your bot something like research the top three competitors to my app. Compare their pricing and key features and then save a structured report to my documents folder. Open claw passes that to your connected LLM claude GPD whatever that you have set up which breaks it into subtasks. Search for the web extract relevant data synthesize it format it and then save the file. Each of those steps runs as a separate skill call on your machine. By the time you wake up, there's a markdown report sitting in your folder. No prompt engineering, no babysitting, no copy pasting from a chat window. That's the fundamental difference. A chatbot gives you an answer. Open claw gives you a deliverable. And then there's the wild stuff. Someone literally told their open claw to build a game before going to bed and they woke up to a working game that was already attracting users. The AI wrote the code, set it up, and the whole thing. People are also using it to run what they call multi- aent setups. Basically, multiple specialized bots working together. One handles marketing, one handles development, one handles strategy, all coordinated through a single chat. It's like having a team of AI employees. And I want to be clear, these aren't demos. These are things that people in the community are actually running in production right now. Okay, I have to be real with you here because as cool as this is, open claw comes with some real risks that you need to understand before you dive in. The first and the biggest one is security. When you set up an open claw and connect it to your email, your calendar, your files, your browser, you're giving an AI agent access to basically everything. And if that's misconfigured or exposed to the internet improperly, bad actors can access it too. And this isn't theoretical. Researchers have found over 30,000 exposed OpenClaw instances on the public internet. People who just set it up and left a port open without realizing it. Within minutes of a honeypot going up, there were already automated scanners probing it. There's also something called prompt injection where a malicious web page or email can secretly give instructions to your AI agent without you even knowing it. And your bot might just follow these instructions. Even Microsoft's security team published a blog post about this recommending you never run OpenClaw on your primary personal account or on a device with sensitive data. So what's the right way to do it? A few things. One, run it on an isolated server, not your main computer. Two, use a dedicated phone number for WhatsApp, not your personal one. Three, log down who can send messages using an allow list. And four, run the built-in security audit that comes with OpenClaw regularly. It's not that OpenClaw is bad, it's just that this is powerful and the powerful tool needs to be handled responsibly. And that's OpenClaw. Is it perfect? No. Security is still a big concern, but is it powerful? Absolutely. But is it one of the most genuinely exciting tools I've come across in a long time? 100%. I'm going to be doing a full hands-on setup video next, walking you through exactly how I set this up on Cloud Server and connected it to WhatsApp step by step. So, if you want to know about it, let me know in the comments. And I would love to know what would you use OpenClaw for. Drop it in the comments, too. If this video was useful, a like really does help this channel grow and helps more people find this content. All right, I'll see you in the next one. This is Anja. Bye-bye.

videos.title: Builders Unscripted: Ep. 1 - Peter Steinberger, Creator of OpenClaw
videos.description:
Builders Unscripted spotlights the stories behind real projects and the mindset that makes them possible: you can just build things. 

Prior to joining OpenAI, Peter Steinberger sat down with Romain Huet, Head of Developer Experience, to talk about OpenClaw, his journey in open source, and how he builds with Codex.

videos.title: OpenClaw Use Cases That Are Actually Insane
videos.description:
Visit https://lumalabs.ai/ailabs20 to try the Dream Machine for free!
Community with All Resources 📦: http://ailabspro.io/
Video code: V44

We tested OpenClaw use cases that turn it into the ultimate clawdbot for developers. From openclaw skills like API cost monitoring and overnight builds to moltbot-style automation, here's how to use openclaw to run your entire dev workflow.

Our team ran an openclaw setup on a Mac Mini for weeks to push its limits as a developer tool. OpenClaw's creator rece
videos.transcript: Ever since OpenClaw got released, many people have started using it as an all-rounder personal assistant, letting it run their entire personal and professional life. But how can it fit into a developer's workflow? As a developer, there are a lot of tasks that need constant monitoring and controlling. It gets tedious and even a missed oversight could lead to huge disasters. So our team pushed OpenClaw to see how many developer tasks it could actually handle for us. We set it up on one of our Mac minis, built different workflows around it, and let it run for weeks. Now, OpenClaw's creator did join OpenAI, but that shouldn't matter because not much has changed for the project itself. And after weeks of testing, we're starting to understand why OpenAI wanted the person who built this. If we have hosted our websites on Versel or any other hosting platform, we can also monitor it with OpenClaw and let it report and fix the dependencies on its own without needing our input. With React apps, there are cases when the dependencies get upgraded because their libraries often get vulnerabilities. Meaning upgrading to the latest patch is critical for ensuring app security like we saw with the React server vulnerability a few months back. So in such cases, we used to manually update the dependencies and push the fix to the source control we were using. I let openclaw manage this as well. We created a dependency maintenance cron job that frequently checks the repo code and the dependencies. It followed the guidelines we had provided for managing the dependencies and reported back on the Discord channel with updates. So with this in place, we had it run every 12 hours. And once it ran, we got updates on Discord that there were new dependency issues and that it tested a safe lock file refresh, which means it updated dependencies to the latest stable versions without breaking changes. It also reported the things that needed our attention, which in our case were the baseline quality checks like linting and testing, which needed our input to fix so that it can push the patch on GitHub. Once the execution completed, it upgraded the dependencies and updated to the latest version on the platform. Since our team has a YouTube channel, we actually have a Discord server where we all talk about different ideas. For a channel like ours, our team needs to be up to date at all times with the latest releases in order to test things out properly from different angles. Our team was already using Mac Minis inside our setup on which we were testing and developing stuff. So, one of us suggested we should use one for the open claw, letting it act like a server within our setup. We used one of them to set up OpenClaw and created a chrome job that researches through our sources for ideas and compiles research targeted towards the tools we usually use in our workflow. It also provided video angles and explained how to use each idea. So with this setup, our team starts our days with a detailed research report in our dedicated text channel. This way we can do our daily briefing on the tech news, what the latest releases were, what the big changes were, and all of the resources clearly organized with different video angles that we can benefit from. It also provided the source links for each piece of news so that we can dig deeper on each item. And this was just one angle of research that we can do with OpenClaw. But this idea can be extended much further than this to research tools, competitors, and much more. When working with cloud providers like Google, AWS, and others, it often happens that we get higher bills than expected because of an incorrect configuration of a VM or any misconfigured load. and we only get to know the bill when we get the invoice unless we monitor it manually using the cloudatch consoles of the respective platform. But with open clause abilities, it's possible to automate that as well. So our team created a skill for an API cost watchdog which checks the budget using the CLI tools of the cloud provider we were using for our project. We had it placed inside the skills folder in theopenclaw folder and the skill was recognized on the dashboard. We created a cron job using openclaw's chat interface and tked it to monitor our API keys and the cloud consoles. We asked it to report back any anomaly on the channels we had configured for it which were WhatsApp and discord. Using this we were able to get a message alert on our discord that the resource usage had spiked in the past 60 minutes and had almost doubled because a certain service had a burst of retries. It also gave actionable steps on what we can do right now to prevent the spike. This setup gave timely reports preventing the bills from climbing further. Now, this skill along with other resources can be found in AIABS Pro. For those who don't know, it's our recently launched community where you get readytouse templates that you can plug directly into your projects for this video and all previous ones. If you found value in what we do and want to support the channel, this is the best way to do it. The links in the description. We can also use OpenClaw for monitoring any hosted app. We had an application that we had already hosted on Versal, but we needed to ensure the security of the application and make sure there was nothing wrong on the server's end that could be harmful to our app. For that, we set up a heartbeat system that performs frequent health checks. When we provided it with the domain of our app, it automatically ran frequent checks to monitor the website's uptime and security issues. It also scanned the website server logs using the credentials we had set up for Versal and checked for cross-sight scripting and SQL injection ensuring that the application remained secure. This way it sent us updates on the configured channel on Discord where it gave us the overall health check of our application in terms of security uptime and average response time as well. It also provided some actionable security hardening steps that would make the application run properly such as adding security headers. But before we move on to the more interesting parts, let's have a word from our sponsor, Luma AI. For developers and technical creators, the primary bottleneck in generative video has always been temporal consistency. Luma addresses this by shifting Dream Machine from a simple generator to a comprehensive production suite. It begins with pre-production tools like boards and Brainstorm, which allow for structured asset organization and visual iteration before a single frame is rendered. At the core is the new Ray 3PI, which generates native 1080p video four times faster and three times cheaper than previous iterations. This efficiency is critical for rapid prototyping and iterative workflows. However, the true production value lies in the advanced control features. Character reference finally solves identity persistence, locking subjects across different shots, while modify enables complete environment or lighting swaps without altering the original physics or motion. This is essential for building consistent narratives. Use the link in the description or scan the QR code to try the full suite yourself. Aside from hosted app monitoring, you can also monitor the application for SEO performance, which is important to make your site visible across search engines. So for that, we actually created another heartbeat check to monitor the hosted application for SEO where it periodically ran multiple checks on the app and ensured that the app is indexable, contained all the required items for a website, and that robots.txt txt and the sitemap are reachable. Once it ran, it sent a full report on how our application was performing so far in the context of SEO and reported all the findings. It also gave the fixes that we need to implement in our app to make the site more visible and rank higher in search results such as fixing the route to sitemap, adding in metad description and adding proper tags. The thing about OpenClaw that stands out is its ability to run on its own for long-term tasks and execute them independently without needing much supervision. This makes building with openclaw easier because it handles testing and bug fixing without explicit input from us which makes shipping end to end smoother. In addition, we can also connect multiple models to openclaw and create multiple agents set up using the models most suitable for each task. So this is the exact reason people have been building and shipping products by letting openclaw coordinate and handle everything on its own. We gave the PRD for the app we had planned to build as an instruction to openclaw in chat and asked it to create the app. Once it set off, it first needed approval in OpenClaw for running the tools and once approved without needing any input from our side. It just built the app, pushed the repo to GitHub, deployed it on Versal, and gave us the links. We also got a summary of its working in the chat. This website was built with the combined power of Codeex and the Gemini 3 model using the best capabilities of each model as needed and match the requirements in the PRD exactly. Also, if you are enjoying our content, consider pressing the hype button because it helps us create more content like this and reach out to more people. Claude code is a strong coding agent, but the one area where Gemini has an edge over it is in image generation capabilities. Open Claw can be used to bridge the gap between the two, letting us use the power of both combined. Now, we previously created a skill for using Google's nanobanana image generation abilities where we talked about another workflow in which you can get free image generation with claude code. You can check out that video on the channel. But OpenClaw also comes bundled with a similar skill called Nano Banana Pro as well, which just needs an API key to enable it. We tested the native skill as well and saw how well it compared to ours. Once the skill was enabled, we used the OpenClaw, provided it the directory path of where the app should be, and asked it to use Claude code to implement the website, used the Nano Banana Pro skill to create images that fit into the application, and place them in the public directory so that Claude can access the images and use them on the landing page. Once it got the approval to proceed, it started the implementation of the application. It broke down the large task into subtasks instead of giving a large prompt to Claude and implemented them step by step, giving us updates on the progress of the implementation and continuously committing to Git as well. In the meantime, it also generated the images that fit with the application. One thing to do before using cloud code via OpenClaw is to either preset the permissions that would be needed to create an application like we did in the settings.json inside the.cloud clawed folder or ask openclaw to run using the dangerously skip permissions flag. This is because openclaw tends to get stuck when clawed code asks for permissions before implementing anything and openclaw has no means of allowing the permission on its own causing the session to end in a timeout. This way the whole website was created just by coordinating nanobanana and claude code together generating images that fit directly into the website flow and allowing us to use both of these tools together. If you're working with a lot of people and you send cold emails to sell your product, you can use OpenClaw to automate this as well. OpenClaw handled the research of leads and possible clients. We were looking for some potential developers who were working on LLM applications so that we could reach out to them and get their reviews on our product. The best place to look for such developers is GitHub. So, we gave it instructions to scrape the GitHub trending page and get all the top ranking users who are working in the same area and have their email and contact information public. We set this up as a chron job and scheduled it to run at 9:00 a.m. every morning. This way, when we begin our day, we return to email drafts already prepared in our account. Open Claw saved all the potential clients to a folder in the documents folder and created draft emails only instead of actually sending them because it needed our cross-checking before sending to the people on the list. These drafts were solid and were exactly built the way we instructed. We gave access to our Google account via the GOG CLI which comes bundled with the OpenClaw installation. We just needed to enable the required APIs from the cloud console and add the credentials from there. Once configured, it was able to access our Gmail. And when the chron job ran, it created three draft emails, including the style we had specified in detail when setting up the chron job via the openclaw chat. Like emails should be casual and conversational and contain a soft CTA. This way, we were able to reach out to multiple people through cold emailing to expand our reach. And since OpenClaw runs on its own all the time as long as the device is on, it works well as a personal assistant. This is because we can configure it with multiple tools like we did by connecting it to the Google Workspace CLI for all Google products. You can also connect it with many other platforms because it comes builtin with multiple skills such as Apple reminders, Gemini, GitHub, and many more, most of which are already preconfigured. You just need to enable them or provide the API key for them to work. With this setup, we've actually been using it as our personal assistant. We set up a heartbeat that scans important emails on every cycle. We did this because a lot of emails from newsletters often cause important emails to get missed when your inbox is crowded. This heartbeat instructs OpenClaw to prioritize emails marked as important and ignore promotional emails and newsletters. It scores emails based on the criteria defined in the Chrome job, identifies actionable items from each, and updates them on the configured channels, be it WhatsApp or Discord. So when we check, we only get email updates from Google Workspace for the items that actually need our attention. We can even ask OpenClaw questions related to calendar events or schedule tasks directly from chat messages. There are often times when we had to use clawed code, but we were away from our devices. It used to be frustrating because we were unable to access what we had in claude code and had no way of accessing it remotely. With OpenClaw, this gap also got bridged because now we just tell our OpenClaw setup to go to the directory where we have what we need and run Claude code to do whatever we want. As we mentioned earlier, OpenClaw is unable to interact with Claude's permission prompts on its own and get stuck indefinitely. So, we either preallow the permissions we know will be required like create, write, and edit in the Claude settings or explicitly ask it to run with the dangerously skip permissions flag. Once that's in place, we asked Claude code to run a research in a directory we used to manage our research tasks and return the complete summary to us. This opened up opportunities for pushing changes in the codebase, reviewing PRs, and fixing production errors, all from our chat applications, even when we are on the go. Now, these were the different ways you can use OpenClaw. But in order to use it effectively, there are certain things you need to be careful of before installing this in the first place. We talked about this in detail in our previous video on this tool. you might end up seeing that video on the end screen, so you can just click on it instead of looking it up. That brings us to the end of this video. If you'd like to support the channel and help us keep making videos like this, you can do so by using the super thanks button below. As always, thank you for watching and I'll see you in the next one.

videos.title: OpenClaw Full Tutorial for Beginners: How to Setup Your First AI Agent (ClawdBot)
videos.description:
In this video I walk you through a complete OpenClaw (ClawdBot) install from scratch — covering deployment on a dedicated device, Telegram bot setup, Open Router API keys, security hardening, and Brave web search integration. I also share my strategy for your first few days so you don't waste tokens trying to do everything at once. Whether you're brand new to OpenClaw or you tried it and gave up, this is the setup guide to get you sorted.

Need help setting this up? https://plugnclaw.com/

🛠️ Hi
videos.transcript: Hello legends. In this video, I'm going to show you how to install OpenC Claw from scratch. I'm going to show you how to deploy it in a safe way. And I'm also going to give you a bunch of tips and tricks on how to use it for the very first few days so you can get the most value out of it. I'm finding that a lot of people fall into two buckets. One, they're either too scared to or they don't know how to install OpenClaw. And two, they do install OpenClaw, but they get too excited. They try to do too many things with it all at once. And then they ultimately waste a lot of time, a lot of tokens, and then they lose their motivation, and they stop using it. So, in this video, I'm going to take you through a brand new install of OpenClaw. I'm going to show you how to connect it up to your Telegram. I'm going to give you a couple of security prompts that will help you secure your deployment even better. The OpenClaw agent is actually able to scan a device that it's on. Understand how you deployed and make some recommendations on how to just harden that deployment. I'll show you how to set up your API key so you can use different AI models. I'll show you how to set up your web search. I'll give you an overview of all the different MD files and how the very first conversation works when you first deploy your agent. And then finally, I'll give you a couple of tips and tricks on how best to use OpenCore for the very first few days. So instead of trying to do absolutely everything all at once, I recommend the scaled back approach where you spend the first couple of days just getting a feel for things and then brain dumping all of your goals, your challenges, whatever tasks, whatever things you're working on to your OpenClaw agent and then asking it to help set up the very next steps for you. So OpenClaw is pretty straightforward to install. You don't want to install it on your main computer where you're logged into all of your accounts, you have any secret information like files, documents or passwords because if your open claw does get compromised, then it can access all those files on a computer. So really what you want to do is get a dedicated device and that could be an old laptop or a computer you have lying around. It could be a Mac Mini, it could be a Raspberry Pi, which is what I use, or it could be a VPS, so a virtual server. If you're using a virtual server, OpenClaw doesn't recommend using a third-party one-click image. So, a one-click deployment is something like Digital Oceans button over here where you can click this and then it spools up a custom configured version of OpenClaw directly on a server. And same thing with Hostinger. You just click on this one-click deploy button and then you have a deployed OpenClaw on their server. So, the upside of using a one-click image is that it's very easy to get started. But the downside is that over time you might want to change some of the configurations. And if you're using someone else's preconfigured version of OpenClaw, it might just be difficult or maybe impossible to change some settings later on. So, so the recommendation is just to start from a blank slate and then do it yourself. So, I'm going to be using a Raspberry Pi for the demo today. And to get started, you just need to copy this command and then open up a terminal on a device that you want to install OpenClaw. So, for me, I'm on my Raspberry Pi. Okay, so I'm going to open up my Raspberry Pi, open up a terminal, and then paste in this command. This command is going to do absolutely everything for you. It'll first look at your device and make sure you got all the prerequisites, and if you don't, it'll just download them. Second, it'll download and install OpenClaw, and then third, it'll finalize the installment, and then it boots up the onboarding process. Now, a suggestion here is that I would not get bogged down on all the different settings that you can choose. I would just do the MVP approach. Just get the bare minimum in place. Use OpenClaw for a couple of hours, maybe a day. Begin to understand exactly where you want to use it, how you want to use it, what do you want it to do for you, and then when you know uh more about what you want to do, wipe OpenClaw from your computer, restart the onboarding process, and then be even more deliberate and specific with the configuration and security settings. OpenClaw even mentions in their security document that there's no perfectly secure setup, and the goal is to be deliberate about who can talk to your bot, where the bot is allowed to act, and what the bot can touch. So only after you use openclaw for the first time and you begin to understand its magic. Only then can you actually know what you want to do with that and then be very deliberate with the options you choose. So let's keep going and say yes we understand the security risks and let's opt for manual onboarding. Let's keep local gateway accept this directory. And for here my recommendation at least for the start is to use something called open router. And if you haven't heard of Open Router, this is a provider that gives you one single API key and then access to pretty much every single AI model that you'd ever want to use. You can access all the OpenAI models, all the Google models, all the anthropic models, and then you get access to these open source models. And the benefit of using these open- source models that are very cheap to run. It's 30 cents per million input tokens and $110 per million output tokens. And this is said to be on par with models like Claude called Opus 4.6. And the comparison here is that for Opus 4.6, 6. It's $5 per million input tokens and $25 per million output tokens. After you create an account from Open Router, go into the credits tab, add about 5 or 10 bucks just so you can get started. Then go across to API keys. Create a new API key. I'd recommend to start off by not configuring any skills, but just to take a look. And here you have a bunch of pre-built tools that can plug into your OpenClaw agent. So I'm going to go no, no again and again and then again all the way through to the end. And over here, let's just enable all of these different hooks. Once we've done, let's hit enter and click yes and hit enter to install the gateway service and then yes for node. Let's hit enter to enable bash. And with that, we complete the onboarding. So at this stage, we have openclaw set up and installed on our machine. Up in the top section over here, you can see the dashboard is ready. Let's just copy the URL link with the token. Copy that and then paste it into your terminal. And here you have your openclaw dashboard. Let's test if this works by sending in a message. Hi there. And here's our response. Now, this conversation is very important. As you can see, we're basically asked to help the agent understand who it is, who we are, and what kind of goals we have. Now, every time you deploy a new agent for OpenClaw, so right now, this is one agent we have, but we can technically go into the agents tab and then just deploy another agent for research, another agent for writing, whatever, whatever use case you need. Every time you deploy a new agent and have your very first conversation, they're always going to have a similar experience like this, asking who you are, who they are, and what their purpose is. So, at this stage, you want to spend 5 or 10 minutes just initially having this conversation with the agent. If you do what I said before where I'm like, just install it first, begin to understand what it's capable of doing for you, and then you begin to understand what you want to do with it. Same thing goes for this conversation. Just get the first conversation out of the way. The important thing is that the agent actually goes across to its own files. So over in the agents tab, if we click on the files over here, we got a bunch of MD files. So this is just marked down. It's just a format of a file like a doc or a PDF, but in this case, it's just MD. So some of the files that we have here are the agents MD file is just the instruction set for the agent whenever it's having a conversation with you. The very first run, it's told to actually use the bootstrap MD. So the bootstrap MD is just that first onboarding conversation where it's been prompted to say, "Hey, figure out who you are, who you're working with, what the goals are." Um, and to basically help build some personality and character for this agent. You have the solemn MD, which is like the personality character. You have the identity MD, which is the agent's identity. So once you tell it exactly who it is, how you want it to operate, it'll fill this out. And then you have the user MD. The user MD is essentially who you are to the agent. So you just give it your name. Uh you can give it your uh time zone that you're in, any special notes about communicating with you, and some more context around projects that you want to create. Over time, uh you'll actually find that you'll evolve whatever files you have here. If you create a research agent, you might tell create a research.md file for you and just start putting in different bits and pieces of research that you're working on. Now these agents are able to edit those files whenever you want or they want. So after you have this first conversation, if you find in two days time that you want to update like how the agent actually communicates with you, which is definitely reasonable, you can tell the agent to go back and just update its own configuration file. So I just finished my initial conversation with OpenClaw. I defined who I was, who the agent was, and what our goals were. And then I reset our session so we can start fresh. Let's now test out the Telegram connection to make sure it works. I'm going to go back to our botfather and open up the chat. Since it's the first conversation we're having, let's go and start the combo. And here we have the message from our agent. Now, my preference is to just use Telegram for all the communication. I don't really like using the dashboard to send and receive messages. Plus, it's on my phone. And literally, wherever you are, you can just access and speak with your agent. Now, the very next thing you want to do before actually going away and giving your agent tasks and starting to see exactly what's possible, I recommend you run a couple of these prompts just to harden security of your setup. The first prompt is basically saying, "Hey, can you please run a security audit of our setup, so of our device and our deployment of OpenClaw, and let me know if we have any issues that we should fix." And since the agent knows how OpenClaw was built and has access to different commands for OpenClaw, like security audits or health checks, it's able to look at your device and then recommend some changes. I recommend you actually read the response and understand what the agent's talking about. And if you agree with everything it said, you can just reply back and say, "Can you please fix everything?" The next command you want to run is around redacting sensitive information from leaking in logs. This is another security feature that you can do in the back end of OpenClaw. There's actually a specific feature where when you're having a conversation, sometimes the metadata of the conversation might have an API key or a specific user ID that you don't want to leak out into the wild. So, we're just asking OpenClaw to enable that feature and just make sure that we're redacting that sensitive information. And this is another really good scan to do. As you can see from default, some of these tokens were shared as plain text. So then the agent went through here and then it made some recommendations on how we can beef up our security and then including down here as well. So you can just reply back to the agent and say, "Yes, I agree with all your recommendations. Let's do it." Then the agent will go away and then fix everything for you. So at this stage, I'd say that you've doubled the security of your instance just by sending those two very basic prompts. Now we have some breathing room and can start actually figuring out what this agent can do for us. This first part is going to be a relatively personal experience where you're going to be prompting up the agent based on your specific tasks and goals, but I'm going to give you a couple of tips and pointers on what you could possibly do. So, the first thing is we want to give our agent the ability to search the web. So, we're going to install the Brave API key. The two default options are using Brave Search or Perplexity for Brave Search, which is what I'm using right now. You get a bunch of free API credits that you can use. I definitely recommend starting with this or since we're using open router already to run our claude sonnet model. You can use open router to use the perplexity web search as well. Let's go across to Brave and get our API key. After you create an account, you don't have to upload any credits. Right now on this plan, you get one request per second, which is actually pretty good. You can just let your agent know to slow it down its internet requests and you get 2,000 requests per month total. So, this is quite a high capacity. Let's click on add an API key. Give it a name and hit add. Let's copy that key. And we can either go back into the agent conversation, give it the API key and say, "Hey, can you please set up Brave web search?" Or we can go with these instructions and copy this command. Back into your terminal, you can keep going on from the previous session you had. Or even if you start a new terminal session, just hit enter. Let's leave it as local and let's enable web search for Brave. Just get that API key one more time. Paste it into here and hit enter. Let's enable web fetch. And it's complete. Now, let's just make sure it works. So, I'm going to get the agent to hit the Brave API and let us know if it was successful or not. Awesome. So, now we have web search. So, let's set up our first daily task. So, I'm asking the agent in plain English, hey, can every day at 8 a.m. you send me the latest AI news? Now, a tip here is that I would just start with something. Start with something small. And typically, I will run this automation or a task or whatever I'm doing for a couple of rounds. So, maybe like a couple of days. And as you go on, you can just get the agent and say, "Hey, you got me the AI news. Can you also let me know the weather or can you actually give me a research report or can you give me some YouTube video ideas?" So, as you go through, you can just tell the agent to optimize whatever task it's doing. Then you can go back and change the scope of work by itself. Now, the final tip that I want to give you is the use of MD files, markdown files. So, for the first few days that you use OpenClaw, it's going to be super exciting and most likely you're going to try and do absolutely everything all at once. A very common thing that people do is, hey, I've got one agent. Why don't I just deploy another two or three agents and you start getting them to do tasks for me. The difficult thing about that is that you don't fully know exactly what you can do with OpenClaw. Instead of like wasting tokens, wasting your time doing unnecessary things, for the first few days, I recommend just sending a bunch of voice notes across to your OpenClaw and explaining here's the things that I'm thinking about. Here's what's on my mind. Here's a couple of goals that I want to achieve. And then piece by piece get your open claw agent to start building out separate MD files. For example, a research.m MD and then to put the research tasks that you mentioned from your conversations or travel.m MD. And if you ever speak about wanting to travel overseas or do things locally, it's going to start putting those tasks and placing them in those MD files. So then over a couple of days, you'll naturally build out two or three really solid MD files with a bunch of tasks immediately embedded into them. So then you can come back to your primary agent and just say hey based on the MD files that we have can you spin up multiple agents and then it'll be very easy for you to configure those agents and then give them tasks so they can hit the ground running. Now my next steps are to be building out a bit more of a series about openclaw. So my next video will be about building an agent team and also building out command center where you can actually communicate with multiple team members on different tasks and projects. If you want to stay up to date with that video, please subscribe to my channel, drop a like on this video, or drop a comment saying that you're excited to see the next one.

videos.title: OpenClaw Full Course: Setup, Skills, Voice, Memory & More
videos.description:

In this full OpenClaw course, I walk you through everything from initial VPS setup to advanced configuration, skills, memory, voice integration, and automation. We’ll cover Docker, gateway access, SSH, model configuration and cost optimization, Telegram integration, speech-to-text, coding skills, markdown system files like IDENTITY and USER, SOUL.md, HEARTBEAT.md, cro
videos.transcript: Today I'll be giving you a full comprehensive tutorial on how to set up OpenClaw so it is productive, efficient, and actually helps you get something done. I'm going to go through skills, memory, the different files you need to configure, voice mode, groups, all of the different things that actually allow you to get some value out of this tool and that I learned the hard way spending now over probably 60 70 hours configuring my own open claw instance. So with that said, let's get into it. So, first I do need to give you a quick disclaimer about security. And also just generally want to mention that in this video I'm going to be pretty thorough. I'm going to be very detailed. I'm going to try to actually teach you something new. So, while it will be pretty long, there's a reason for that. And I would suggest that you're going to get a lot more value out of a video like this than a quick 10-minute setup where they're just blitzing through all of the steps and you have no idea what's going on. Anyways, that brings us to security. Now, while what I'm going to show you in this video in terms of setup is secure, if you want to go kind of extreme and make sure that you're very secure, I have a full 50inute long video that breaks down every step you can take to harden and secure your OpenClaw instance. So, I'll put that on screen right here. I'm not going to repeat everything in that video because again, it's already filmed and it's there for you to watch. And I'm going to assume generally that you already know how to set up open claw in terms of getting at least access to the open claw gateway or accessing it from the terminal or telegram. I will repeat those steps but I will go through that quickly and focus most of my effort on all of the skills integrations connections etc. Now with that in mind the general best practice here is to treat openclaw like an untrusted virtual assistant. OpenClaw previously known as moltbot or clawdbot is the latest AI model agent you can run locally on your own PC that is autonomous connects to MCP like Zapier https://bit.ly/4tgTFlK and runs 24/7 and was just recently supported by OpenAI as open source project. With the logo of a lobster, and created by Peter Steinberger, it is written in typescript and people are buying mac mini's to run it non stop!

In this video I'll explain how to set it up and start using it on your own system. We will co
videos.transcript: This is OpenClaw, an autonomous AI agent that runs 24/7 on your own PC or VPS. Written in Typescript by Peter Steinberger, it now has official backing from OpenAI who are supporting this as an open- source project. So, for this video, I'm going to show you exactly how to set it up, configure it, and start using it right now. To install OpenClaw, we'll head down to this quick start, copy this command, and paste it into our terminal. The installation takes 1 or 2 minutes. Once installed, we can do a quick configuration to get it up and running. If it's the first time you're using it, I recommend just simply using quick start. Next, you'll need to connect up an AI model such as OpenAI or Anthropic. I'm going to use Anthropic with its API keys. You can do that easily by heading over to the claw developer website. Click start building, get an API key, and create a key. Make sure you give it a label like open claw, then copy over this key back into the terminal under the anthropic API key section. You can then pick which model to use. I'm going to use Claude Opus 4.6, but be aware that OpenClaw can rack up a lot of costs very quickly. Now, you can configure a channel to communicate with OpenClaw, such as Telegram, WhatsApp, or others. For now, we'll skip this and we'll do it later in the video. Next, we can set up some skills. I'll also skip this section since it's not necessary and we can do this later in the video, too. There are a few more requests here like using Google maps or nano banana for videos or even notion or openai for image gen but all of these we'll skip so that we can get it up and running. This takes us to the very last option. You can now select how you use openclaw whether it's through the terminal or through a web UI. If you selected the terminal one you can immediately start talking to openclaw right here in this instance. Selecting web UI takes you to this website here with a session token and it lets you start talking with OpenClaw just like you would with chat GPT. Let's initialize it. First, it's going to ask us about identity. We'll need to set up a name for OpenClaw as well as who we are. Here, I'm just going to ask it to refer to itself as claw and myself as Adrien. You can go into further detail about how you want it to act and this will be saved in memory so it can always be referred back to when it's interacting with you. You can see that in action here where it's writing to file different things like its own identity, working with the user, etc. Now let's create a channel from openclaw to WhatsApp. We can do this in the terminal or during the setup process. If you've already installed openclaw, you can run openclaw space channels space ad to configure this new chat channel. I'm going to set up WhatsApp and then I'm going to set up telegram so you know what the process is like. WhatsApp is pretty easy. You simply link up a QR code to the phone number you want OpenClaw to use. Once you scan it, it'll ask you if it's your personal phone number or a separate phone number, which is recommended if you're setting up OpenClaw to run as its own agent. You'll also need to fill out your own personal phone number here so that it knows which phone number to message you on. With that done, now let's set up Telegram. Run OpenClaw space channel space ad. I'm going to select the default primary account and follow the instructions here to create a Telegram bot, which I can utilize for OpenClaw. On your phone, head to Telegram. You'll want to create a new message to the following contact, which is atbotfather. Select the first one with the verify tag. And in here, you'll want to set up a new bot for openclaw to use. Add the following command, which is for/ newbot. Give your telegram bot a custom name that's unique. I'm going to call mine Adrian Tuarog's clawbot. You'll be given a token that you'll need to provide to open claw. So, make sure you copy this and then paste it here in the terminal. With that done, we've got Telegram now set up. Scroll down to finished. And this should complete our setup for OpenClaw. This will let you interact with OpenClaw directly from your phone as you're out and about. And these chat sessions will be directly in sync with your own PC back at home. If you use a different provider like WhatsApp or Discord, it'll function similarly. Now, let's connect up some tools and apps for OpenClaw to use. I'm going to see if it can connect up to my Zap year MCP server since I already have lots of apps preconfigured and connect it through there. It's also a little bit more secure since I can select exactly what I want OpenClaw to be able to access. OpenClaw already has given me directions to set it up. All I have to do is head to the Zapier MCP server, create a new one with a type as other, and then connect up the tools which I want to use. Right now, I just want to do some testing. So, I'll only connect it up to Gmail to find emails and maybe also to create drafts. I don't want it to start deleting or sending emails on my behalf. This is the benefit of being able to have a little bit more granular control over the types of apps you give OpenClaw access to. Now, to establish the connection, I'm going to generate a token. And rather than me manually configuring this like a programmer, I'm just going to copy paste all of this content straight into OpenClaw and hope that it sets it up all for me. And after a few seconds, here's the response. It's all configured and it's also got the available tools I've given it access to, such as getting emails and creating graphs. Let me test that this is working by getting the latest five emails from my inbox. And here they're presented in a nice chat layout. Before we go further, I definitely think it's worth mentioning how important security as well as risk is when you're using OpenClaw. For example, the very first thing that you need to do when you install OpenClaw is to accept those risks. Now, when it comes to installing skills or using things like tools, then I've read up to 17% of them are already honeybots made by hackers to leak information. Pretty scary if you think about it. It's why I'm using things like Zapier MCP and other MCP tools to act as a man in the middle as a trusted source rather than connecting it directly. Even just using keywords like help me set up my Gmail might ask you to pass that information in and it might not even be Gmail that you're connecting to. So definitely make sure that you are very specific about what you give it access to. Additionally, be aware that OpenClaw will start using up a lot of API requests and this can amount to a lot of money. Even day-to-day use just for me testing has amounted to a few dollars and this could be vastly more if you're running it 24/7. This is where running local models on your own PC might be beneficial through something like Olama, which has already added a configuration file here for OpenClaw. If you haven't got Olama installed, now's the time to do that. Let me show you how to quickly configure this with a good model to utilize with OpenClaw. Copy the Olama launch Open Claw configuration into your terminal. The first recommended model will actually be a new one here called GLM47 Flash. And this one's around 25 gigs. I'm going to select to utilize it and download it, which is going to happen in the background. This model might be a little bit big and this is why a lot of people are buying Mac minis to run their open claw in isolation on their own local PC. Of course, you don't have to do it that way. You can always run it on a VPS connected to something like open AI or claude. If you want to know more about how to customize this, there's great documentation on the open claw website. But now that we have it all set up, if you check console by running open claw space models, you'll see that the lama model is now the default model just over here with some fallback models for anthropic over here. The next thing we'll do is open up the default folder of open claw so we can view it in VS code or cursor. To do this, just copy over the main workspace which is normally open claw just as I've presented here inside of cursor. This workspace will give you access to everything from the agents and their configuration sessions and logs as well as things like automatic chron jobs, our MCP connection to Zapier and much more. What's useful about this workspace is that you can now sync it to GitHub and that way you always have a copy of it in case you want to copy it over to a different PC or run very similar versions of it. Open Claw looks like a really interesting project. I'm going to use it for 30 days building multiple agents and seeing how they actually help me on a day-to-day basis. I hope you guys enjoyed this video. A big thanks to Zapier for sponsoring it.

videos.transcript: OpenClaw is the most important AI software I have ever used. It has fundamentally changed how not only I work, but I live. And I'm even going to show you how I set up OpenClaw to be self evolving. It is wild. So, let's get into it. All right. So, first, what is OpenClaw? I've made multiple videos about it, so I'm only going to go over this briefly. If you want a more basic guide, check out my previous videos. OpenClaw is an open-source framework that allows you to take the best AI models and builds an incredibly personal AI assistant that is capable of accomplishing almost any task that you can do on a computer. And what makes it really special is that it learns from you. It evolves over time and you can access it using the chat apps that you already use. WhatsApp, Telegram, text messaging, Slack, all of them. OpenClaw also has a pretty awesome personality that you can craft to be the exact personal assistant that you want it to be. And this is done through two main files, identity.md and soul.md. So here's my identity.md file. And so this is a slight evolution on what comes by default, but you could basically make it anything you want. And then the soul is where you actually give it its true personality. This is where you describe things like how you wanted to answer you, how concise, how verbose, how personal, how formal. All of this is defined right here. I even gave it a humor style style rules, when to dial it down. When it's talking to me, I want it to be more personal, more like a friend. When I invoke it from Slack in the context of my business and other people can see it, I want it to be more formal, more like a colleague. All of this is defined in soul.md. Also, as I mentioned, it has a very capable memory system, and there's a few different flavors of it. I'm actually using the default memory system for now. There's a new out-of-the-box memory system called QMD byite, the founder of Shopify. He just has a bunch of time on his hands to build memory systems for OpenClaw. There's also things like Super Memory, which are external services, which I personally just prefer to keep it all on my local machine. This is how the memory system works. You have a bunch of conversations. You go back and forth with your bot. It takes daily notes. It saves it in the memory folder with the day as a markdown file. It starts to store it in memory. MD as distilled preferences. Then the next session, it will actually read the file and it updates the identity files per your memories. Now, we're also vectorizing all of these files so we can easily do rag search against them. And if you don't know what that means, don't even worry about it. It happens all automatically for you. It just allows your open claw to be able to query all of this natural language, all of these conversations that you've had very easily. So, some examples of what it can actually remember. videos.title: OpenClaw Use Cases that are Actually Helpful! (ClawdBot)
videos.description:
More new leads + 1500 followers + ALL my resources
👉 https://www.skool.com/buildroom/

Summary ⤵️
Learn how to set up OpenClaw the RIGHT way — not just install it, but actually configure a team of AI agents that run your business on autopilot. I spent $200+ stress testing this so you don't have to.

The AI Agent Dream Team FREE!
👉 https://www.notion.so/The-Ultimate-Gu...

⏱️ Video Timestamps:
00:00 - How to set up OpenClaw AI agents
01:10 - How to plan your agent system
02:06 - How to use differ
videos.transcript: OpenClaw is the most powerful AI agent framework in the world right now and it's about to replace your entire workflow. While everyone else is still prompting chat GPT one message at a time, you could be running a specialized team of AI agents that work for you 24/7, remember everything, and cost significantly less than a full-time employee to operate. The problem is there are a lot of tutorials out there showing you how to set up Open Claw on a Mac Mini or VPS, but nobody's showing you how to actually get it to do anything. I spent over $200 in the last 48 hours stress testing the system so you don't have to. In this video, I'm going to show you how to set up the chat model so you don't break the bank, how to deploy a self-healing team of AI agents that communicate with each other, and how to set up the infrastructure that lets your AI run on autopilot. And if we haven't met yet, my name is Duncan Rogoff. I'm a former art director for brands like Apple, PlayStation, and Nissan. And I now run a six-figure AI agency. In under 12 months, I've grown a following of over 110,000 people, and I lead a community called the Buildroom where more than 2,000 are already using AI to grow their audience and generate leads for their business in under 3 hours per week. So, focus in, close all your tabs, and let's build. So, here we are inside of OpenClaw, and it's not always the sexiest to look at, but today I really want to cover like the most impactful things that I've set up that actually help me run my business. These are a couple things like communicating with it, using Telegram, setting up different LLM models to handle different tasks so I can save on cost, like using cron job to just tackle tasks like a daily morning briefing or saving this to GitHub or things like that. And then a breakdown of how I've set up my AI agent orchestras we have the orchestrator agent that communicates with all the other agents which really allows us to be as efficient as we possibly can be.
