videos.title: Kimi Claw: OpenClaw AI Agent Running in Your Browser (Setup Tutorial & Demo)
videos.description:
Kimi Claw is a browser-hosted OpenClaw AI agent that runs entirely in the cloud — no local setup, VPS, or always-on machine required.

In this video tutorial, I walk through what Kimi Claw is, how to set it up, and how its persistent agent model differs from traditional chat AI like ChatGPT or Claude. We’ll look at scheduled automations (cron jobs), ClawHub skills, and how hosted OpenClaw agents can run tasks and workflows for you in the background.

Kimi Claw is still in beta — especially aroun
videos.transcript: Open claw agents are starting to move into the browser. Yesterday, Moonshot announced the launch of Kimmy Claw. In this video, I'll walk you through what it is, how to set it up, and a few important security things to understand before using browser hosted agents like this. If you're not familiar, OpenClaw is an open- source AI agent framework. It gives you an always assistant with long-term memory that can run tasks, learn your preferences, and automate workflows. The catch has always been that running Open Claw means you need something always on. So, people have been buying dedicated Mac minis or spinning up VPS instances. It works, but it's not exactly plug-andplay, and Kimmy Claw is designed to remove that. It's a cloud hosted version of OpenClaw powered by Moonshot's Kimmy K2.5 model. It runs entirely in your browser, so there's no local install and no server config. Kimmy deploys and manages the agent for you with persistent memory and 40 GB of cloud storage. So, if you've been wanting to run AI agents without managing infrastructure, this video will show you exactly how to do that with Kimclaw. As you can see on screen, we can deploy Kimmy Claw with one click. There's no complex setup and it's online 24/7. It's configured with the Kimmy K 2.5 thinking model and it has readyto-use skills and runs across multiple messaging apps. So, we get the benefits of OpenClaw but in the browser with one click. You can also see it says link existing open claw. So, if you're already an OpenClaw user and you've got an instance setup, you can also link that here rather than doing the one-click setup in the browser. But for this video, we're going to choose the one-click setup option, and I'll show you how that works. Once we click create, we'll see this popup, which says Kimmy Claw is starting up. It takes about a minute, so you'll let this run to set up your instance of Kimmy Claw. Once it's ready, we'll see this. And this is your Kimmy Claw agent workspace. At this point, you've got a fully running OpenClaw agent hosted in Kimmy's cloud and accessible from anywhere just by logging in to your Kimmy account. So instead of running OpenClaw locally on a machine that needs to stay online, this agent is now always on by default, you can interact with it just like a chatbased assistant, but the key difference is that this agent has persistent memory and long-term state. So it can store information, run tasks, and maintain context across sessions. So here's a really good example of what makes an OpenClaw agent different from a normal chat assistant. When I asked Kimmy Claw to send me AI news every morning, it didn't just reply with information. It actually created a scheduled task, what's called a chron job, that runs automatically in the background. A cron job is basically a timer based automation. It tells the agent to execute something on a schedule. In this case, searching for AI news and delivering a summary at 9:00 a.m. every day. Because the agent is hosted in Kimy's cloud, that job runs even when I'm offline or the browser is closed. The agent itself is always on. This is fundamentally different from chatting with something like chat jbt or claude in a browser tab for example. Those systems respond when you ask them something but they don't persistently run tasks for you over time. Once you close the tab nothing continues executing after that. Here the open claw agent actually owns and runs the automation. So it behaves more like a personal cloud worker rather than a chat session. On the back end, Kimmy stores this scheduled job with your agent state and their hosted OpenClaw runtime executes it on schedule, then delivers the result back to you. So, you can think of Kimmy Claw as running a persistent agent process on your behalf in the cloud. That always on execution is really the key difference between openclaw agents like this and traditional chatbased AI. And once you start connecting this agent to external channels like Telegram or WhatsApp, you can see how this turns into a really powerful automation workflow. For example, that daily AI news wouldn't just exist here in the chat. It could also be delivered directly to your phone every morning by the agent itself. I'll cover those integrations in a deeper video, but this just gives you a sense of what persistent agents like this can actually do. One question you might have on persistent agents is how do we actually manage multiple scheduled tasks? Right now, it's straightforward. We've just got one task scheduled, but what happens when we've got 10, 15, 20 different tasks all scheduled? How do we stay on top of exactly what the agent is doing so that we can stop or continue specific tasks? Right now in Kimmy Claw, which is still in beta mode, there isn't yet a dedicated UI panel that lists all cron jobs or automations. So you don't see a task list here in settings the way you might in something like Zapia or chat GPT. If we open up the settings tab, you can see we can only really edit our bot name. That's all that we can really do in the settings at the moment. The workaround for this at the moment is we can just chat with Kimmy Claw. We can say what scheduled tasks do you have running. It should then return and tell me exactly what's running. We can see here daily AI news. That would be how you can then manage tasks. So if you want to have a look at all of the tasks Kimmy Claw is currently running, you can just prompt it in this chat window and then you can say for example turn off the daily AI news task and that's how you can then manage those tasks. And one important thing to understand with Kimclaw right now is that it's still very much in beta especially around integrations and external connectors. As we can see here in the Kimmy Claw user guide, integrations like Telegram are configured directly through the agent conversation where you create a bot, provide the token to Kimmy Claw, and then restart the agent to complete pairing. So connectors are possible, but in the current hosted environment, there isn't much visible UI showing how those credentials are stored or managed behind the scenes. That means anything you configure, including tokens, memory, tasks, and files, ultimately lives inside Moonshot's hosted agent runtime rather than on your local device. So, it's best to think of Kimmy Claw less like a local OpenClaw instance you fully control and more like a SAS automation platform that's running an agent on your behalf in the cloud. Because of that, my recommendation right now, especially while the platform is still maturing, is to keep workflows relatively self-contained. Things like scheduled tasks, research, and browser level automations are a great fit. As Kimclaw evolves and exposes clearer integration controls, and documentation around how secrets and connectors are handled, that's when it will make more sense to expand into deeper external automations. Another really cool feature of Kimmy Claw is that it has a direct integration to the Clawhub skill library. This is a library of community-built skills that your agent can discover and install on demand. So instead of manually browsing plugins, you can just ask the agent to find and install a relevant skill for the task you want to do. Think of a skill essentially as just a detailed saved prompt. So you can use that as a skill, install that and just call on that using a command anytime you want to run that prompt or detailed workflow. I'll jump over to the chat UI and show you how it works. So we're back in the chat UI. You can see I've just said find and install a suitable skill for AI news analysis. Kimmy Claw has then searched the claw hub skill library for suitable skills. It has identified AI news collector skill. It looks perfect for your needs. Let me search any AI specific analysis skills. It's then done some more research and decided that the aforementioned skill is the one that I need. It's then installed that successfully after hitting a rate limit and then it's explained exactly what the skill does. You can see how it works here. It uses a multi-dimensional search strategy and then it gives you a clear output format. So it has now got this skill installed. So what I can do now is say schedule AI news daily at 9:00 a.m. and use this skill and then it will deliver it in this format. So that's an example of how you can install skills to help power your workflows. So that's a look at Kimmy Claw, a browser hosted openclaw agent running in Moonshots cloud with persistent memory, scheduled automations and installable skills through clawhub. The key idea here is that you're no longer managing infrastructure or running agents locally. Kimmy is hosting and operating that agent runtime for you. It's still early and very much in beta, especially around integrations and connectors, but even at this stage, you can already see how persistent agents like this start to enable real automation workflows. In future videos, I'll dive deeper into skills, integrations, and how Kimclaw compares to self-hosted OpenClaw and other agent platforms. If you're exploring AI agents or automation workflows, this is definitely a space to watch. So, make sure you subscribe if you want to see more tutorials on Kimmy Claw and Open Claw.

videos.title: My Multi-Agent Team with OpenClaw
videos.description:
I ordered a Mac Mini just to run OpenClaw — and set up a team of 4 AI agents to help me run my business. In this video, I walk through every decision I made: hardware, security, costs, chat interface, multi-agent architecture, and what I'm actually using my agents for.

This isn't a plug-and-play setup. I'll show you the strategic and technical questions I had to figure out, how I landed on each answer, and the rough edges I ran into along the way.

👇 *Your Builder Briefing (free)*
https://build
videos.transcript: Well, sitting on my desk is a new Mac Mini that I set up just for the purpose of running my team of AI agents using OpenClaw. I've got a developer, a marketer, a project manager, a system admin. They each have their own personality. They've got a queue of tasks tracked in this custom dashboard that I built and I'm chatting with them in Slack just like I would with my real team members except they're agents powered by OpenClaw and various large language models. What a time this is. But, you know, getting this up and running was not a plug-and-play situation. Over the past week and many late nights, I had to figure out my answers to question after question, some technical, some strategic, like, should I order a new Mac Mini or can I run OpenClaw on a VPS? And what's this going to cost me in API tokens? And could I use my Claw Max plan? And what chat tool is best for my agents, Telegram or WhatsApp or Slack? Should I have it power one agent or can I set up a team of agents? and am I going to need a custom dashboard for managing them? And let's not forget about security. What should my agents be able to access? And how should I think about safeguards? And most importantly, what's my use case here? What will I have my team of agents actually do for me? So today, I'll share where I've landed on all of those questions, and I'll show you my setup for all of it. You know, to be honest, I didn't see the appeal of OpenClaw at first. Back when it was called Claudebot and then Moltbot and it was buzzing around Twitter a couple weeks ago, everyone was talking about having their agent respond to their emails for them or book flights or order takeout. And I don't want or need an AI agent in my personal life and I don't even want it to manage my calendar. But then I started thinking about a real challenge that I've been having in my business and how setting up OpenClaw could help me solve it. You know, I run this YouTube channel and Builder Methods Pro on my courses and a weekly newsletter. And thanks to things like Claude Code, of course, building things has never been easier. But building it is only half of what I do. I develop training content. I manage a publishing pipeline. And I oversee my membership business. But lately, I've been bottlenecked. There's so much more that I want to create and deliver. If only I had the bandwidth. In my past businesses, I solved this by hiring real teams and building processes to help us scale. And that worked, but the overhead was real, too. So, when I gave OpenClaw another look, I asked a different question. Not do I want a personal assistant, but what if this could fill roles on my team? And now, I'm convinced that this paradigm, autonomous agents with defined roles running on their own machines, I think this is here to stay. Now, OpenCloud is just the first generation of what I think will be much bigger. So, I want to be figuring this out now, and maybe this video can help you get started, too. If you're new here, I'm Brian Castle. I help builders stay ahead of the curve with AI. And every Friday, I send my builder briefing. That's a free fiveminute read where I give you my no hype take on making this transition to adopting AI. You can get yours by going to buildermethods.com. And if you're serious about leveling up, check out builder methods pro where you can join our community and get training for builders. All right, so what actually is OpenClaw? It used to be called Cloudbot and then Moltbot. And how is this actually different from how you might use cloud code or any other agent? The core of OpenClaw is what's called the gateway. That's a process running on a machine, which shouldn't be your personal machine, but we'll talk about security in a moment. The OpenCloud gateway can run tools. It could use a browser. It can execute bash scripts. Of course, cloud code can do a lot of that, too. But what makes OpenCloud different is that it's always on. It maintains a persistent workspace with memory and session logs. So you can chat with your agents through Telegram or Slack and delegate tasks that they can do on their own in the background. So that's a fundamentally different paradigm from you personally managing cloud code sessions in your terminal. OpenClaw is closer to having teammates who do their work on their own workstations. The first question is where should this thing run? Now I don't recommend you run OpenClaw on your daily driver machine. You don't want to give it unfettered access to your files and your accounts. And even if you isolate it with something like Docker, your machine would need to be on and awake 24/7 for your agents to work. So, OpenClaw needs its own dedicated machine. That could be a cloud VPS starting at around five bucks a month. Or it could be a physical machine. Doesn't have to be a Mac Mini. Any kind of computer on your network. Both are valid and a lot of people are doing well with the VPS setups. But I went ahead and I spent the 600 bucks on a new Mac Mini M4. And call me old school, but I like to be able to screen share into it, see the desktop, install things, and manage it visually. And I SSH in too when I just need to run a quick command.
